params.text:
Why complaints matter

It is easy to hear the word “complaint” and feel defensive, but in adult social care a complaint is best understood as a gift. When a person tells us they are unhappy, they are giving us something valuable: honest feedback and a chance to put things right, learn, and improve the service for everyone.

Making a complaint is also a right. The people we support — and their families and representatives — are entitled to raise concerns about their care and to have them taken seriously. A service that welcomes complaints is usually a safer, more open and more trusted service.

  • A complaint tells you where care is falling short, often before it becomes a serious problem.
  • People who feel heard are more likely to keep trusting the service and stay engaged.
  • Complaints that are welcomed rather than resisted lead to genuine improvement.

What the law and regulator expect

Handling complaints well is not just good manners — it is a legal and regulatory expectation. In England this is shaped by the Local Authority Social Services and NHS Complaints (England) Regulations 2009 and by the Care Quality Commission’s Regulation 16: Receiving and acting on complaints. Together they require services to have an accessible way to complain, to investigate fairly, and to act on what they learn.

params.question: A complaint should be seen as a valuable opportunity to learn and improve, not just a problem to be managed.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. A complaint is a chance to learn, improve and rebuild trust — treat it as a gift, not a threat.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Treating complaints as a gift helps us put things right and improve care for everyone.
